records.ety: [AS. āwæcnan, v. i. (imp. awōc), and āwacian, v. i. (imp. awacode). See Awaken, Wake.]
records.morph: [imp. Awoke (<?/), Awaked (<?/); p. p. Awaked; (Obs.) Awaken, Awoken; p. pr. & vb. n. Awaking. The form Awoke is sometimes used as a p. p.]

defs: To rouse from sleep; to wake; to awaken.
quotes: Where morning's earliest ray . . . awake her.
And his disciples came to him, and awoke him, saying, Lord, save us; we perish.

defs: To rouse from a state resembling sleep, as from death, stupidity., or inaction; to put into action; to give new life to; to stir up; as, to awake the dead; to awake the dormant faculties.
quotes: I was soon awaked from this disagreeable reverie.
It way awake my bounty further.
No sunny gleam awakes the trees.

defs: To cease to sleep; to come out of a state of natural sleep; and, figuratively, out of a state resembling sleep, as inaction or death.
quotes: Awake to righteousness, and sin not.

records.ety: [From awaken, old p. p. of awake.]

defs: Not sleeping or lethargic; roused from sleep; in a state of vigilance or action.
quotes: She still beheld, Now wide awake, the vision of her sleep.
He was awake to the danger.
